what_changed: The system went from having no defined basic operations to having a minimal operator identified as the irreducible starting point, after simpler alternatives were tested and ruled out.
Two concepts that were initially treated as separate were shown to mutually produce each other: one generates the other, which in turn enables the first.
A single network configuration was expanded to multiple conditions, and stability of the core property was confirmed across all of them.
The hypothesis that density caused a particular correlation was tested and falsified; the data showed density was not a causal factor.
The hypothesis that structural importance caused a particular correlation was tested and falsified; the statistical evidence did not support the claim.
A correlation measured by a single method was tested across multiple estimation methods; it proved robust in rank-ordering but sensitive to method on absolute level.
Seven metrics with unknown relationships were compared pairwise; most turned out to be redundant, with one metric shown to be a projection of another.
A phenomenon observed in initial measurements was shown to be a binary artifact of how the data was discretized, not a real effect.
Two ways of describing system structure were compared; the topological description proved more fundamental than the specific set description, as different sets could share the same topology.
Two ways of describing structure were compared; preserving distinctions proved more fundamental than the distinctions themselves.
Patterns assumed to be objective structures were shown to depend on both the world and the representation chosen; different representations yielded different patterns.
Causal explanation was tested against other criteria and shown to fail most of the time; other criteria proved more reliable across many domains.
Path independence, previously assumed, was confirmed across all eleven construction orderings tested; different sequences produced the same result.
Four junction points with unknown relationships were shown to depend on each other in a linear chain; no independent junctions existed.
An apparent progression across audits was shown to be a methodological artifact of audit ordering, not genuine conceptual change.
Progression initially assumed to be linear improvement was shown to be oscillation; the system cycled through recurring patterns instead of converging.
The role of a particular mechanism in producing gains was clarified: every observed gain involved it, making it a necessary condition.
Four elements studied separately were shown to require a specific configuration together; removing any one element broke the configuration.
Objects modeled as static things were reconceived as the accumulated trace of past events rather than fixed entities.
A claim based on initial analysis was rescored under re-examination but did not gain additional support; no new explanatory gain resulted.
